---
title: "Curriculum-Trained YOLOv8 for Hierarchical Dental Diagnosis on Panoramic X-rays"
author: ""
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    number-sections: false
    toc: false
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry: margin=1in
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Abstract

Panoramic dental X-rays are complex images containing many anatomical structures, so clinical interpretation is typically performed in stages. Dentists first identify the correct quadrant of the mouth, then determine the specific tooth using the FDI numbering system, and finally diagnose any pathology affecting that tooth. Many existing deep-learning approaches simplify this process into a single end-to-end detection task and train only on the fully labeled portion of the available dataset. In this study, we revisit the DENTEX 2023 challenge dataset and propose a three-stage curriculum learning pipeline using YOLOv8m-seg that reflects the clinician’s diagnostic workflow while making use of all hierarchical annotation levels in the dataset. In Stage 1, the model is trained to detect quadrants using 1,398 images. In Stage 2, those learned weights are fine-tuned for tooth enumeration using 1,339 images. In Stage 3, the enumeration model is further adapted to a four-class diagnosis task using 705 fully labeled images with instance-segmentation supervision. The curriculum model is compared with a single-stage baseline trained on the same fully labeled data with matched hyperparameters. We also ask how sensitive the diagnosis model is to realistic image degradations (blur, noise, motion) and whether training-time augmentation closes the gap, and whether region-focused preprocessing at different anatomical scales (full image vs. cropped sub-regions) improves performance and robustness.

# Introduction

Dental caries and periapical (dental root) disease remain among the most prevalent chronic conditions in the world, and panoramic radiography is often the first imaging modality clinicians use to observe a patient's full condition [@who2022oral, @schwendicke2020dental]. A single panoramic X-ray contains a full picture of the thirty-two teeth. Any subset of teeth may exhibit pathology and reading such an image is a time-consuming, high-cognitive-load task which is susceptible to varying interpretations across observers [@krois2019deep]. Computer-aided interpretation of dental radiographs is therefore a worthwhile approach with modern computer vision. The object classes are well-defined, the annotation conventions are standardized (the FDI system), and even small automation can translate into reductions in clinician workload and diagnostic inconsistency.

Dental diagnosis on panoramic X-rays is also harder than the benchmarks modern detectors were built on. The images are grayscale and geometrically distorted, the appearance of each tooth is strongly conditioned on its position in the arch, and the DENTEX 2023 challenge [@hamamci2023dentex] released only 705 fully-labelled training images, which is small enough that even fine-tuning risks over-fitting given the class imbalance.

A second feature of the DENTEX release is that it is not a single labelled dataset but a hierarchy of three partially-overlapping ones. In addition to the 705 disease-annotated images, the benchmark releases 693 quadrant-only images and 634 enumeration-level images. The original DENTEX challenge [@hamamci2023diffusion] used the disease tier as the sole training signal and treated the coarser tiers as supplementary data for optional pre-training. We treat them instead as a curriculum. Learning to find a quadrant is strictly easier than enumerating a tooth, which is strictly easier than diagnosing pathology on that tooth, and each coarser task is a well-defined sub-problem of the next, so weights learned at one stage are directly reusable at the next.

We therefore develop a three-stage curriculum in which each stage uses YOLOv8m-seg and differs from the next only in label granularity, training subset, and initial weights. To isolate the contribution of curriculum training from augmentation strength or schedule, we also train a single-stage baseline with identical hyperparameters on only the 705 fully-labelled images. In addition to this comparison, we investigate two questions that bear on practical deployment: how realistic image degradations (blur, noise, motion) affect diagnosis performance and whether degradation-aware augmentation recovers the loss, and whether region-focused preprocessing at different anatomical scales improves performance and robustness.

# Related Work

**Deep learning on dental radiographs.** Early deep-learning applications to dental imaging focused on binary caries classification from intra-oral bitewing radiographs, using convolutional networks trained on a few thousand cropped per-tooth patches [@lee2018caries, @krois2019deep]. These studies established that modest CNNs can approach dentist-level agreement on caries detection when the input is tightly cropped and the task is framed as classification rather than detection. Extension to panoramic radiography, in which the input is an uncropped whole-mouth view, required moving from classification to detection, and a subsequent line of work applied off-the-shelf detectors (Faster R-CNN, YOLOv3, YOLOv5) to tooth enumeration and limited-class pathology detection [@silva2018automatic, @tuzoff2019tooth]. The DENTEX 2023 benchmark [@hamamci2023dentex] consolidated this line of research into a standardised multi-tier challenge and introduced a diffusion-based hierarchical detector, HierarchicalDet [@hamamci2023diffusion], which jointly predicts quadrant, tooth number, and diagnosis. HierarchicalDet treats the three annotation tiers as heterogeneous supervision for a single multi-head model rather than as a curriculum.

**Curriculum learning.** Training on progressively harder examples or objectives improves convergence and generalisation in deep networks [@bengio2009curriculum]. The task-level variant, in which the model sees progressively finer-grained objectives rather than harder instances of the same objective [@pentina2015curriculum, @wang2022curriculum], is the version we adopt. This approach has precedent in radiology [@jimenez2019curriculum] but, to our knowledge, has not been applied to panoramic dental radiography.

**YOLOv8.** YOLOv8 [@jocher2023yolov8] is an anchor-free single-stage detector. We use the `yolov8m-seg` variant because its segmentation head provides native polygon supervision, which matches the polygon masks shipped by the DENTEX disease tier.

**Hierarchical label structures in detection.** Most work that exploits label hierarchies does so at inference time through hierarchical softmax [@redmon2017yolo9000] or multi-head detectors [@deng2014large]. Our approach exploits the hierarchy at training time, decomposing the target task into strictly easier sub-tasks and training sequentially. A side effect is that the intermediate quadrant and enumeration checkpoints are themselves usable outputs.

# Dataset

The DENTEX dataset was released as part of the MICCAI 2023 Dental Enumeration and Diagnosis Challenge. It consists of panoramic dental X-rays collected from three clinical institutions in Turkey, annotated by practising dentists using the FDI Two-Digit Notation. The dataset is partitioned into three hierarchical training tiers, a single validation split, and a single test split, summarised in Table 1.

| Split                   | Images | Annotation level                         | Format             |
|-------------------------|--------|------------------------------------------|--------------------|
| Train (quadrant only)   | 693    | Quadrant (4 classes)                     | COCO JSON          |
| Train (enumeration)     | 634    | Quadrant + tooth position (8 per quad)   | COCO JSON          |
| Train (disease)         | 705    | Quadrant + tooth + diagnosis (4 classes) | COCO JSON + masks  |
| Train (unlabelled)      | 1,571  | None                                     | Images only        |
| Validation              | 50     | Quadrant + tooth + diagnosis             | COCO JSON          |
| Test                    | 250    | Quadrant + tooth + diagnosis             | LabelMe JSON (TR)  |

: DENTEX dataset partitions and annotation formats.

**Annotation hierarchy.** The three training tiers are nested. The enumeration tier's annotations include quadrant labels as a proper subset, and the disease tier's annotations include both quadrant and enumeration labels. The disease-tier JSON exposes the nesting through three separate category fields, whereas the coarser tiers use a single `category_id`. This schema inconsistency is minor but demands care during preprocessing.

**Test-set idiosyncrasies.** Each of the 250 test images is paired with a LabelMe-format JSON whose `label` strings are Turkish and follow the pattern `<code>-<diagnosis_term>-<FDI_tooth_number>`, for example `1-çürük-36` (caries on tooth 36). Diagnostic terms (`çürük`, `küretaj`, `kanal tedavisi`/`lezyon`, `gömülü`) map to our four classes. Non-diagnostic terms (`sağlam`, `çekim`, `kırık`) are skipped, which discards 131 of the 1,600 test annotations. LabelMe polygons are retained as segmentation supervision for mask-metric computation.

**Class distribution of the final target task.** The disease-tier training set exhibits substantial class imbalance (Table 2). Caries is by far the most common class, accounting for 62% of the 3,529 annotated objects across 705 images. Periapical Lesion is the rarest, at 4.5%. On the test set the balance is closer, with Caries accounting for roughly half of the 1,469 mapped annotations, but the training imbalance implies that per-class performance is likely to degrade most sharply on Periapical Lesion.

| Class              | Train objects | % of train | Test objects | % of test |
|--------------------|---------------|------------|--------------|-----------|
| Impacted           | 604           | 17.1       | 221          | 15.0      |
| Caries             | 2,189         | 62.0       | 747          | 50.9      |
| Periapical Lesion  | 158           | 4.5        | 236          | 16.1      |
| Deep Caries        | 578           | 16.4       | 265          | 18.0      |
| **Total**          | **3,529**     | **100.0**  | **1,469**    | **100.0** |

: Disease-class object counts in the stage-3 train and test splits (after test-set mapping).

# Preprocessing Pipeline

Because Ultralytics YOLOv8 does not natively consume COCO or LabelMe JSONs, we built a three-script pipeline (orchestrated by `preprocessing.py`) that produces, per curriculum stage, the standard Ultralytics layout of `images/{train,val,test}` and `labels/{train,val,test}` plus a dataset YAML.

**Training conversion** (`scripts/01_convert_training_to_yolo.py`) reads the three tier JSONs and writes one YOLO `.txt` per image with polygon vertices normalised to $[0,1]$ and the class ID drawn from the tier-appropriate category field. Stages 1 and 2 concatenate the coarser tier with the disease tier and re-map disease-tier labels to the coarser taxonomy.

**Test conversion** (`scripts/02_convert_test_to_yolo.py`) parses the 250 LabelMe JSONs, mapping Turkish diagnosis terms to class IDs and skipping non-diagnostic terms.

**YAML generation** (`scripts/03_generate_yamls.py`) writes one dataset YAML per stage specifying paths, class count, and class-name mapping.

`preprocessing.py` verifies that expected JSONs and image directories exist and that emitted label counts match image counts.

# Methods

## Model

All stages use the `yolov8m-seg` variant of YOLOv8 from Ultralytics. Stage 1 initialises from COCO-pretrained weights, and stages 2 and 3 initialise from the previous stage's best checkpoint. The medium variant is chosen over the larger `l` and `x` variants because the disease tier only has 705 training images and the larger variants over-fit in preliminary runs.

## Curriculum

The curriculum consists of three sequential training runs at a fixed 640-pixel input. The stages differ in the dataset, the class count, the initial weights, and the learning-rate schedule.

**Stage 1, quadrant detection.** 1,398 images (693 quadrant-only plus 705 disease-tier), four quadrant classes, initialised from COCO weights. Because the task is coarse and the data volume is largest, we use the highest learning rate in the curriculum (1e-3) and a 3-epoch warmup.

**Stage 2, tooth enumeration.** 1,339 images (634 enumeration-tier plus 705 disease-tier), eight tooth-position classes, initialised from the stage-1 checkpoint. The learning rate is halved (5e-4) and the warmup is shortened to 2 epochs so the stage-1 representation is not disturbed too aggressively.

**Stage 3, diagnosis detection.** 705 disease-tier images, four diagnosis classes, initialised from the stage-2 checkpoint. The task is no longer positional (the model already knows where teeth are) but appearance-based. The learning rate is reduced to 3e-4, copy-paste augmentation (probability 0.1) is added for the rare classes, and early-stopping patience is raised from 20 to 25 epochs because the dataset is smaller.

## Baseline

The single-stage baseline uses the same architecture, augmentations, batch size, optimiser, and early-stopping patience as stage 3. It differs only in that it initialises from COCO weights (not a stage-2 checkpoint) and uses a 1e-3 learning rate (matching stage 1 when starting from COCO). Any performance gap between the baseline and the stage-3 curriculum model is therefore attributable to the curriculum itself.

## Degradation robustness (RQ3)

Panoramic radiographs in practice are not always of research-dataset quality. Patient movement, low-dose protocols, and sensor artefacts introduce blur, noise, and motion-like distortion that a model trained on clean DENTEX images may not handle well. We probe this sensitivity and ask whether training-time exposure to the same degradations closes the gap.

**Degradation operators.** Three corruption families are applied to the input image at a small grid of severity levels spanning mild-but-noticeable to clearly-degraded-but-still-plausible. Gaussian blur simulates defocus and head movement, additive noise simulates low-dose sensor read noise, and a motion operator simulates rotational movement during the panoramic sweep.

**Protocol.** The stage-3 curriculum model and the baseline are evaluated on the clean test set and on degraded versions of it. A degradation-augmented variant of stage 3 is then trained with the same operators applied stochastically and re-evaluated on both clean and degraded test sets. The clean-to-degraded delta in mAP@0.5 is the primary robustness measure.

## Region-focused preprocessing (RQ4)

A detector that sees the full mouth at a fixed 640-pixel input shares a limited resolution budget across all thirty-two teeth, but pathology is local. Cropping to a smaller anatomical region trades global context for per-tooth pixel density. We compare full-image diagnosis against cropped scales.

**Anatomical scales.** The full panoramic image is the reference. Candidate crops are quadrant-sized regions (from the stage-1 detector or ground-truth quadrant polygons) and per-tooth regions (from the stage-2 detector or ground-truth tooth polygons).

**Protocol.** For each scale, a dedicated diagnosis model is trained on crops at that scale and evaluated on the 250-image test set under the same crop procedure. Per-scale mAP@0.5, mAP@0.5:0.95, and per-class AP are compared against the stage-3 full-image model. The best region-focused model is additionally evaluated on the RQ3 degraded test sets, so anatomical scale and degradation exposure can be examined jointly.

## Training configuration

All runs use AdamW with weight decay 5e-4 and a cosine schedule decaying the initial learning rate to 1% of its starting value. The loss is the standard YOLOv8 composition (DFL and CIoU on boxes, BCE on classes, BCE plus dice on masks). Augmentations are HSV jitter, horizontal flip, vertical flip (probability 0.1), and full mosaic. Copy-paste augmentation (probability 0.1) is enabled only for stage 3 and the baseline. Training runs for up to 100 epochs per stage with early stopping on validation mAP@0.5. Table 3 summarises the stage-by-stage configuration.

| Parameter              | Stage 1   | Stage 2   | Stage 3   | Baseline |
|------------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Initial weights        | COCO      | Stage 1   | Stage 2   | COCO     |
| Training images        | 1,398     | 1,339     | 705       | 705      |
| Number of classes      | 4         | 8         | 4         | 4        |
| Epochs (max)           | 100       | 100       | 100       | 100      |
| Batch size             | 8         | 8         | 8         | 8        |
| Input resolution       | 640       | 640       | 640       | 640      |
| Initial LR             | 1e-3      | 5e-4      | 3e-4      | 1e-3     |
| LR decay factor        | 0.01      | 0.01      | 0.01      | 0.01     |
| Warmup epochs          | 3         | 2         | 2         | 3        |
| Optimiser              | AdamW     | AdamW     | AdamW     | AdamW    |
| Weight decay           | 5e-4      | 5e-4      | 5e-4      | 5e-4     |
| Copy-paste probability | 0.0       | 0.0       | 0.1       | 0.1      |
| Early-stopping patience| 20        | 20        | 25        | 25       |

: Per-stage training configuration.

# Experimental Setup

## Evaluation metrics

We report standard COCO-style metrics, mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.5:0.95, computed separately on boxes (B) and masks (M). Per-class AP is reported alongside the mean so the training-time class imbalance (Table 2) can be evaluated class by class. All metrics are computed by Ultralytics' `model.val()` on the 250-image test split using the stage-3 YAML.

## Research questions

The evaluation addresses four research questions.

**RQ1, does the curriculum outperform a matched single-stage baseline?** We compare the stage-3 curriculum model against the baseline on mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.5:0.95 (box and mask) and per-class AP. Because hyperparameters are matched, any gap is attributable to the curriculum itself. The prior expectation is that Periapical Lesion, being rare (4.5% of train) and visually subtle, is the class most likely to lag.

**RQ2, which stage contributes most to final performance?** We evaluate each of the three curriculum checkpoints on the 50-image validation split, re-labelled to the stage's taxonomy, and track how quadrant, enumeration, and diagnosis mAP evolve across the pipeline.

**RQ3, how does realistic image degradation affect performance, and does degradation-aware augmentation recover the loss?** We quantify the drop in mAP@0.5 on clean vs. degraded test sets for the stage-3 curriculum model and the baseline, and the recovery provided by training-time augmentation, reported per operator and severity level.

**RQ4, does region-focused preprocessing at different anatomical scales improve performance and robustness?** We compare region-focused diagnosis models against the full-image stage-3 model on the clean test set and on the degraded test sets from RQ3 to probe interactions between anatomical framing and corruption.

## Implementation and reproducibility

Training and evaluation are implemented in two Jupyter notebooks that invoke per-stage Python scripts wrapping `ultralytics.YOLO.train()`. Random seeds are fixed. Checkpoints and metric CSVs are saved under `models/checkpoints/runs/<stage>/` so every claim in the results section traces to a specific `results.csv`.

# Results

## RQ1, curriculum vs. baseline

*(To be written once stage 2, stage 3, and the baseline finish training.)*

## RQ2, per-stage contribution

*(To be written once all three curriculum stages finish training.)*

## RQ3, degradation robustness

*(To be written once the degradation experiments complete.)*

## RQ4, region-focused preprocessing

*(To be written once the region-focused experiments complete.)*

# Discussion

*(To be written after results.)*

# Conclusion

*(To be written last.)*

# References {.unnumbered}
